does: This paper introduces Calibrated Collective Oversight (CCO), a method for maintaining human oversight over autonomous AI agents that may exceed human capabilities. CCO aggregates multiple auxiliary scoring functions into a penalty that measures deviation from a conservative baseline action, penalizing high-utility actions proportionally to how much overseer concern they generate. The approach combines insights from Attainable Utility Preservation with Conformal Decision Theory to calibrate this conservatism online, guaranteeing that undesirable outcomes stay below a user-specified target threshold with finite-time statistical bounds and no distributional assumptions. The authors demonstrate their approach on a modified version of SWE-bench, showing that weaker overseers can effectively constrain agent behavior.
connects: This work addresses the scalable oversight problem, a central challenge in AI safety arising from the difficulty of supervising systems with capabilities exceeding human understanding. It builds on prior approaches like Attainable Utility Preservation that aim to preserve baseline behaviors, while addressing limitations in existing scalable oversight methods that rely on restrictive assumptions, heuristic techniques, or lack sequential decision-making guarantees. The paper integrates Conformal Decision Theory, a framework from statistical learning that provides distribution-free guarantees, to provide principled coverage guarantees in the oversight setting. This connects to broader lines of work on reward learning, human-in-the-loop control, and behavioral constraints for autonomous agents.
enables: This work enables practical deployment of autonomous agents in high-stakes domains where human oversight is infeasible but necessary, particularly for extended planning tasks. By providing finite-time statistical guarantees without distributional assumptions, CCO could support the development of agents that humans can confidently deploy despite capability gaps. The approach opens directions for improving collective oversight mechanisms through better auxiliary scoring functions, investigating sample efficiency in conformal calibration for real-time oversight constraints, and extending the framework to multi-agent settings where oversight authority is distributed. It also suggests practical tools for aligning agent behavior with human values when direct human evaluation of every action is impossible.
